topic.title: Local-First Software
topic.description: A starter workspace for local-first software trade-offs, sync architecture, and maintenance watchpoints.

canonical.role: Durable canonical pages and maintenance syntheses live here. This remains the source-of-truth layer for the topic.

working.role: Working notes, maintenance sequencing, review artifacts, and audit follow-ups stay visible here so the topic can be resumed without guesswork.

projection.role: The Obsidian projection is an additive working layer for maps of content, compact bundles, and day-to-day reading. It is not the truth layer.

notes: 3 starter corpus files define the first bounded reading set for Local-First Software.
The corpus is intentionally small so the initial atlas, working notes, and context packs stay compact and debuggable.

files.title: Local-first core ideas
files.description: A bounded starter note on local copies, offline use, and sync as a first-class system concern.

files.title: Sync engines
files.description: A starter note on replication models, conflict policy, and schema-change cost.

files.title: Maintenance risks
files.description: A starter note on migration pressure, device drift, and when watchpoints should graduate into syntheses.

starterPages.title: Local-First Software
starterPages.summary: Local-First Software is the canonical entry page for this topic bootstrap. Use it to record the shortest durable explanation of what the bounded corpus currently seems to say.
starterPages.purpose: The canonical entry page for the topic bootstrap.
starterPages.role: The durable entry point for first-pass understanding.
nextSteps: Use [[Local-First Software current tensions]] to keep live uncertainty visible instead of hiding it in the article layer.
Use [[Local-First Software reading paths]] to keep the first note bundles compact and reusable.

sections.heading: Why this topic matters
lines: This page should become the shortest durable explanation of Local-First Software once the corpus has been reviewed and the strongest claims have stabilized.

sections.heading: Starter boundaries
lines: Keep the first pass bounded. A small, explicit corpus produces better starter pages than a broad pile of undifferentiated intake.
Treat this page as durable knowledge, not as the place where unresolved tensions or maintenance sequencing should accumulate.
Use concept pages for sync architecture and migration pressure once those patterns recur often enough to deserve durable coverage.

sections.heading: Evidence to strengthen next
lines: Promote only the highest-confidence recurring claims into this page.
Leave disputes, unresolved questions, and watch logic in the dedicated working surfaces until they stabilize.
A durable explanation of local-first software should connect local copies, sync cost, and long-term maintenance rather than treating them as separate concerns.

starterPages.title: Sync engines
starterPages.summary: In this starter corpus, sync engines look like the operational heart of a local-first system: they decide how state replicates, how conflicts resolve, and how expensive schema changes become over time.
starterPages.purpose: A concept page that keeps replication and conflict policy visible as a central design pressure.
nextSteps: Use this page to keep replication-model decisions separate from higher-level product framing.
Promote recurring sync-engine trade-offs into watchpoints only when they start changing maintenance behavior.

sections.heading: Design pressures
lines: A sync engine is not just transport. It shapes the cost of collaboration, repair, and schema evolution.
If the sync layer is weakly specified, local-first ergonomics can look convincing in demos while staying fragile in practice.

sections.heading: What to inspect next
lines: Track whether conflict policy is explicit enough to stay out of ad hoc repair playbooks.
Track whether replication choices are starting to dictate migration and device-repair workflows.

starterPages.title: Schema migration pressure
starterPages.summary: The starter corpus frames migration pressure as one of the hardest long-term burdens in local-first software because every local copy can turn a data-model change into an operational event.
starterPages.purpose: A concept page that turns maintenance and upgrade cost into a durable design surface.
nextSteps: Use this page to keep migration and repair pressure visible as part of the topic's durable story.
If watchpoints repeatedly mention upgrade or repair cadence, turn that pattern into a stronger synthesis candidate.

sections.heading: Why migration pressure matters
lines: A local-first system inherits every copy of the data model, so migrations can become a coordination problem instead of a one-time deployment task.
Repair paths, drift handling, and upgrade timing belong in the same conversation as schema evolution.

sections.heading: Signals to monitor
lines: Look for recurring evidence that schema shifts are forcing manual repair or device-specific recovery work.
Look for signs that migration tooling is becoming the hidden boundary between a believable system and a fragile one.

operationalNote.title: Note: What should I revisit next for Local-First Software
operationalNote.summary: This starter operational note keeps the next reading pass concrete. It should stay short, grounded, and closely tied to the maintenance loop for Local-First Software.
operationalNote.purpose: A working operational note that keeps the next-pass checklist visible.
nextSteps: Refresh this note after updating [[Local-First Software maintenance watchpoints]].
If the same checklist item keeps recurring, promote it into [[Local-First Software maintenance rhythm]] or a durable synthesis candidate.

sections.heading: Current checklist
lines: Keep the next reading pass narrow and explicit.
Check whether the bounded corpus still supports the current starter framing.
Promote repeated operational concerns into maintenance watchpoints instead of letting them drift between notes.

readingPasses.title: Provenance pass
readingPasses.description: Use this when you need to inspect the bounded corpus and the future artifact trail instead of only reading the canonical pages.

tensionsSummary: The starter corpus points to a classic local-first tension: the product feels strongest when data is always local and collaborative, but sync policy, migration cost, and repair burden become the real long-term operating surface.
tensions: Elegant collaboration promises versus conflict-resolution reality.
Fast product iteration versus schema migration and repair cost.
tensionImportance: A local-first system can look simple from the top-level product story while hiding most of its real cost inside replication and recovery logic.
If conflict policy stays vague, users pay the price later through repair workflows and trust erosion.
If migration pressure is treated as an implementation detail, maintenance work becomes harder to resume and harder to plan.
openQuestionsSummary: These are the highest-leverage questions if you want this starter workspace to become a serious durable knowledge base instead of a thin concept sketch.
openQuestions: Which sync-engine assumptions are durable enough to keep in canonical pages instead of working notes?
Which conflict or repair signals should become explicit watchpoints?
Which migration concerns deserve their own synthesis instead of staying inside a concept page?

researchQuestions.question: Which sync-engine assumptions are durable enough to keep in canonical pages instead of working notes?
researchQuestions.summary: This question determines whether the topic can move beyond a good starter sketch into durable knowledge about where local-first systems really get expensive.
researchQuestions.whyNow: The topic cannot mature honestly until it knows which sync claims belong in canonical pages and which still need working-note caution.
evidenceToAdvance: The corpus starts connecting local copies, sync choices, and repair burden in a stable way.
reopenTriggers: A new source changes the current framing of sync cost or conflict policy.
provenanceNotes: Start from the canonical entry and Sync engines page, then use Maintenance Triage to check whether the claim set is really stable.

researchQuestions.question: Which conflict or repair signals should become explicit watchpoints?
researchQuestions.summary: This question is still evidence-hungry because the starter corpus names repair burden, but it does not yet show enough repeated operational signals to harden a watch list.
researchQuestions.whyNow: Without explicit watchpoints, local-first maintenance risk stays visible only as a theme instead of an operating surface.
relatedPages: Note: What should I revisit next for Local-First Software
relatedTensions: Elegant collaboration promises versus conflict-resolution reality.
evidenceToAdvance: A context pack can answer migration or watchpoint questions without reopening every source note.
sourceGaps: The starter corpus still needs repeated evidence that the same repair or conflict signal changes operator behavior more than once.
reopenTriggers: A coverage audit or new corpus note starts naming recurring repair events directly.
provenanceNotes: Use Maintenance Triage first, then inspect Provenance And Review if the watchpoint logic still feels under-grounded.

researchQuestions.question: Which migration concerns deserve their own synthesis instead of staying inside a concept page?
researchQuestions.summary: Migration pressure is already a durable concern in the topic, and it is the clearest question that can graduate into a stronger synthesis next.
researchQuestions.whyNow: Promoting this question would give the topic its first real migration-oriented synthesis instead of leaving the issue split between a concept page and maintenance notes.
relatedPages: Note: What should I revisit next for Local-First Software
relatedTensions: Fast product iteration versus schema migration and repair cost.
evidenceToAdvance: The same watchpoint keeps changing maintenance order and deserves durable synthesis treatment.
reopenTriggers: A new source changes whether migration pressure still looks like the main maintenance boundary.
provenanceNotes: Use Schema migration pressure with Maintenance Triage to decide whether this question is ready for its first standalone synthesis.

researchSessions.title: Ground the durable sync assumptions
researchSessions.goal: Decide which sync-engine claims are stable enough to move from starter notes into the canonical topic layer.
researchSessions.summary: This completed boundary pass sharpened the canonical story around sync cost and left the still-provisional repair assumptions in working surfaces.
researchSessions.draftConclusion: The starter corpus already supports a durable canonical story about sync engines as the operational heart of local-first software, but the repair burden still belongs in working notes and watchpoints.
evidenceGained: The corpus repeatedly ties local copies to sync-engine choices and long-term maintenance cost.
The sync-engine concept page can carry more durable weight than a generic starter note.
remainingUncertainty: The topic still needs a stronger synthesis about migration and repair burden before the canonical story should get more specific.
researchSessions.recommendedNextStep: Keep the canonical sync framing stable, then use the maintenance rhythm to decide whether migration pressure should become the next synthesis.

questionStatusChange.reason: The starter corpus now supports an active canonical-boundary question instead of a vague bootstrap uncertainty.

resumeNotes: Reopen this pass if a new source changes the framing of sync cost or conflict policy.

researchSessions.title: Promote the migration playbook
researchSessions.goal: Turn the migration-pressure thread into the first real synthesis instead of leaving it split across concept and maintenance notes.
researchSessions.summary: This active session is the clearest path from starter topic to a more mature workspace because migration pressure already changes how the topic should be maintained.
relevantPages: Note: What should I revisit next for Local-First Software
researchSessions.draftConclusion: The next durable surface should likely be a migration playbook that explains when schema change, repair burden, and watchpoints become operationally significant.
evidenceGained: Migration pressure already influences maintenance order more than most other starter concerns.
remainingUncertainty: The topic still needs one tighter pass on when migration pressure becomes a playbook rather than a concept-page warning.
researchSessions.recommendedNextStep: Use this session to decide whether the migration playbook can be promoted now, then update the maintenance rhythm and open-questions note together.
resumeNotes: Start from Maintenance Triage before reopening the whole graph.
If the pass still feels thin, keep it active rather than forcing synthesis.

researchSessions.title: Collect repeat repair and conflict signals
researchSessions.goal: Decide whether repair burden has enough repeated evidence to justify explicit watchpoints.
researchSessions.summary: This queued session keeps the watchpoint question honest by waiting for repeated operator-facing evidence instead of promoting every starter concern into monitoring logic.
relevantPages: Note: What should I revisit next for Local-First Software
researchSessions.draftConclusion: The current starter corpus names real repair pressure, but it still does not prove that the same signal changes operator behavior often enough to harden into a watchpoint list.
remainingUncertainty: The topic still needs repeated evidence of the same repair or conflict signal recurring across more than one pass.
researchSessions.recommendedNextStep: Wait for another source or audit pass, then compare whether the same repair signal still changes maintenance order.
resumeNotes: Do not turn this into a durable watchpoint until the same repair signal recurs in a clearly operational way.

resolutionSignals: The corpus starts connecting local copies, sync choices, and repair burden in a stable way.
A context pack can answer migration or watchpoint questions without reopening every source note.
The same watchpoint keeps changing maintenance order and deserves durable synthesis treatment.

auditActions.signal: A coverage audit flags a thin or weakly linked area.

auditActions.action: promote the disagreement into a durable tension instead of letting it disappear into proposal history.

auditActions.signal: A watchpoint repeatedly changes what you read next.
auditActions.action: rewrite the monitoring synthesis first, then update the operational note only if the checklist actually changed.

artifactLadder: Source files enter through `source-corpus/` and remain the explicit input set for this topic.
Future normalized files should land in `workspace/raw/processed/` once import and cleanup begin.
Future source summaries should land in `workspace/raw/processed/summaries/`.
Future review proposals should land in `workspace/reviews/approved/` and `workspace/reviews/rejected/`.
Durable canonical knowledge for Local-First Software lives in `workspace/wiki/` and remains the source of truth.
The Obsidian vault is an additive projection for daily work, not a second truth layer.
